---
title: "Simulating Individual Tumor Dynamics and Their Link to Survival: A Two-Stage Approach"
subtitle: "Portfolio project — Pharmacometrics-inspired modeling in oncology"
author: "Juliette Bouli-Mengue"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

## 1. Motivation

In oncology drug development, individual tumor size trajectories (typically summarized as
Sum of Longest Diameters, SLD, per RECIST criteria) are often linked to survival outcomes
(Progression-Free Survival, PFS; Overall Survival, OS) to support dose selection and
efficacy predictions. This notebook demonstrates, on **fully simulated data**, the classic
"two-stage" approach used in pharmacometrics: individual tumor dynamics parameters are
first estimated from longitudinal measurements, then used as covariates in a survival
model.

No real patient data is used here. The goal is to demonstrate methodological understanding
before applying the same logic to real datasets.

### The Tumor Growth Inhibition (TGI) model

We use the biexponential TGI model (Wang et al., *Clin Pharmacol Ther*, 2009):

$$SLD(t) = SLD_0 \times (e^{-g \cdot t} + e^{d \cdot t} - 1)$$

- **g**: tumor regression rate (treatment effect)
- **d**: tumor regrowth rate (resistance)
- **SLD0**: baseline tumor size

```{r libraries}
library(minpack.lm)
library(survival)
library(survminer)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

set.seed(42)
```

## 2. Simulating "true" individual patient parameters

We simulate 200 patients. Each has their own growth (`g`), regrowth (`d`), and baseline
tumor size (`SLD0`), drawn from log-normal distributions to reflect realistic
between-patient variability (parameters must be strictly positive, and biological rate
parameters are typically right-skewed).

```{r simulate-patients}
n_patients <- 200

g_pop      <- 0.020
omega_g    <- 0.5
d_pop      <- 0.010
omega_d    <- 0.6
SLD0_pop   <- 60
omega_SLD0 <- 0.3

patients <- tibble(
  patient_id = 1:n_patients,
  g    = rlnorm(n_patients, log(g_pop), omega_g),
  d    = rlnorm(n_patients, log(d_pop), omega_d),
  SLD0 = rlnorm(n_patients, log(SLD0_pop), omega_SLD0)
)

head(patients)
```

## 3. Simulating survival outcomes

Survival is simulated using a proportional hazards structure: a higher `g` (better
response) protects the patient (reduces hazard), while a higher `d` (faster regrowth)
increases the hazard. This mirrors the clinical rationale that tumor dynamics parameters
carry prognostic information.

```{r simulate-survival}
beta_g <- -15   # protective effect of g
beta_d <- 20    # detrimental effect of d
h0     <- 0.015 # baseline hazard

patients <- patients %>%
  mutate(
    g_c = g - mean(g),
    d_c = d - mean(d),
    linear_pred = beta_g * g_c + beta_d * d_c,
    hazard = h0 * exp(linear_pred),
    true_event_time = rexp(n(), rate = hazard)
  )

admin_censoring <- 104

patients <- patients %>%
  mutate(
    os_time  = pmin(true_event_time, admin_censoring),
    os_event = as.integer(true_event_time <= admin_censoring)
  )

summary(patients$os_time)
table(patients$os_event)
```

A quick sanity check: patients with higher `g` should tend to survive longer.

```{r sanity-check}
cor.test(patients$g, patients$os_time, method = "spearman")
```

## 4. First attempt: sparse sampling design

We first simulate a realistic but relatively sparse observation schedule: a scan every
6 weeks, with 15% multiplicative measurement noise (a plausible level of imaging
imprecision).

```{r first-attempt-longitudinal}
assessment_times <- seq(0, 54, by = 6)
sigma_obs <- 0.15

longitudinal_data <- patients %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  do({
    pt <- .
    tibble(
      patient_id = pt$patient_id,
      time = assessment_times,
      SLD_true = pt$SLD0 * (exp(-pt$g * assessment_times) + exp(pt$d * assessment_times) - 1)
    )
  }) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(SLD_obs = SLD_true * exp(rnorm(n(), 0, sigma_obs))) %>%
  filter(SLD_obs > 0)

ggplot(longitudinal_data %>% filter(patient_id <= 12),
       aes(x = time, y = SLD_obs, group = patient_id, color = factor(patient_id))) +
  geom_line() + geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Simulated tumor size trajectories (first attempt)",
       x = "Weeks", y = "SLD (mm)", color = "Patient") +
  theme_minimal()
```

### Stage 1: re-estimating g and d per patient from noisy scans

We now act as if the true `g` and `d` were unknown, and re-estimate them for each patient
from their noisy longitudinal measurements only, using nonlinear least squares.

```{r first-attempt-fit}
fit_one_patient <- function(df) {
  tryCatch({
    fit <- nlsLM(
      SLD_obs ~ SLD0_est * (exp(-g_est * time) + exp(d_est * time) - 1),
      data = df,
      start = list(SLD0_est = df$SLD_obs[df$time == 0][1], g_est = 0.02, d_est = 0.01),
      lower = c(SLD0_est = 1, g_est = 0.0001, d_est = 0.0001),
      upper = c(SLD0_est = 500, g_est = 1, d_est = 1),
      control = nls.lm.control(maxiter = 200)
    )
    coefs <- coef(fit)
    tibble(g_hat = coefs["g_est"], d_hat = coefs["d_est"], convergence = TRUE)
  }, error = function(e) {
    tibble(g_hat = NA_real_, d_hat = NA_real_, convergence = FALSE)
  })
}

individual_fits <- longitudinal_data %>%
  group_by(patient_id) %>%
  filter(n() >= 4) %>%
  group_modify(~ fit_one_patient(.x)) %>%
  ungroup()

cat("Convergence rate:", mean(individual_fits$convergence, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, "%\n")
```

### Stage 2: Cox model on the re-estimated parameters

```{r first-attempt-cox}
analysis_data <- patients %>%
  inner_join(individual_fits, by = "patient_id") %>%
  filter(convergence, !is.na(g_hat), !is.na(d_hat))

cox_model_v1 <- coxph(Surv(os_time, os_event) ~ g_hat + d_hat, data = analysis_data)
summary(cox_model_v1)

cor(analysis_data$g, analysis_data$g_hat)
cor(analysis_data$d, analysis_data$d_hat)
```

### First result: the signal is diluted

```{r first-attempt-plot}
ggplot(analysis_data, aes(x = g, y = g_hat)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Estimated vs true g — sparse design (6-week scans, 15% noise)",
       x = "True g (hidden)", y = "Estimated g (g_hat)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

**Interpretation.** With this sparse observation design, `g_hat` correlates only
moderately with the true `g` (~0.57), while `d_hat` recovers the true `d` well (~0.92).
Consequently, the Cox model built on `g_hat` and `d_hat` fails to detect a significant
association with survival (p ≈ 0.60 for `g_hat`), even though we know, by construction,
that a strong true effect exists (`beta_g = -15`).

This is a textbook case of **attenuation by measurement error**: when an explanatory
variable is estimated with substantial noise, its estimated effect on the outcome is
biased towards zero, even if the true underlying effect is strong. The asymmetry between
`g` and `d` recovery is itself informative: over this observation window, the regrowth
signal (`d`) tends to dominate the tumor trajectory towards the end of follow-up, giving
the optimizer a clearer signal for `d` than for the earlier, more transient regression
phase captured by `g` — an **identifiability** issue rather than a modeling error.

## 5. Adjusting the observation design

Rather than treating this as a dead end, we adjust two aspects of the *simulated
clinical trial design* — which is a legitimate lever in real protocol planning:

- **Scan frequency**: every 3 weeks instead of every 6, over a longer follow-up window
  (72 weeks instead of 54), giving 25 measurements per patient instead of 10.
- **Measurement noise**: reduced from 15% to 8%, still realistic but less extreme.

```{r adjusted-longitudinal}
assessment_times <- seq(0, 72, by = 3)
sigma_obs <- 0.08

longitudinal_data <- patients %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  do({
    pt <- .
    tibble(
      patient_id = pt$patient_id,
      time = assessment_times,
      SLD_true = pt$SLD0 * (exp(-pt$g * assessment_times) + exp(pt$d * assessment_times) - 1)
    )
  }) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(SLD_obs = SLD_true * exp(rnorm(n(), 0, sigma_obs))) %>%
  filter(SLD_obs > 0)
```

We re-run the exact same two-stage pipeline on this improved design.

```{r adjusted-fit}
individual_fits <- longitudinal_data %>%
  group_by(patient_id) %>%
  filter(n() >= 4) %>%
  group_modify(~ fit_one_patient(.x)) %>%
  ungroup()

cat("Convergence rate:", mean(individual_fits$convergence, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, "%\n")

analysis_data <- patients %>%
  inner_join(individual_fits, by = "patient_id") %>%
  filter(convergence, !is.na(g_hat), !is.na(d_hat))

cox_model_v2 <- coxph(Surv(os_time, os_event) ~ g_hat + d_hat, data = analysis_data)
summary(cox_model_v2)

cor(analysis_data$g, analysis_data$g_hat)
cor(analysis_data$d, analysis_data$d_hat)
```

```{r adjusted-plot}
ggplot(analysis_data, aes(x = g, y = g_hat)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Estimated vs true g — improved design (3-week scans, 8% noise)",
       x = "True g (hidden)", y = "Estimated g (g_hat)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

## 6. Summary of the two-stage results

| Design | cor(g, g_hat) | cor(d, d_hat) | beta_g estimated | p-value (g_hat) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sparse (6-week scans, 15% noise) | 0.568 | 0.919 | -2.67 | 0.60 (n.s.) |
| Improved (3-week scans, 8% noise) | 0.830 | 0.990 | -19.47 | 0.007 (significant) |

**Key takeaway.** The ability to detect a true tumor-dynamics/survival association is
highly sensitive to the quality of the observation design (scan frequency and
measurement precision). This directly illustrates why trial design choices matter for
pharmacometric modeling, independent of whether the underlying biological effect is
strong.

The two-stage approach, however, still has a structural limitation: it treats parameter
estimation and survival modeling as two independent steps, ignoring the uncertainty of
`g_hat`/`d_hat` when they are reused as fixed covariates. This motivates the joint
modeling approach developed in the companion notebook
(`02_joint_model.Rmd`), where the longitudinal trajectory and survival are estimated
simultaneously.
